planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-aerated soil in part shade to full shade; Japanese bird nest fern thrives in the warm, humid, shaded conditions of Gulf Coast and tropical gardens and is outstanding for shade beds, woodland paths, and shaded patios.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; keep the center crown (the growing point) free of debris and avoid wetting it during watering. Asplenium antiquum (Japanese bird nest fern) is a large, elegant fern from Japan and East Asia producing broad, undivided, strap-shaped, glossy, medium-green fronds 18–30 inches long arranged in a spreading, vase-like rosette around a central cup — the 'bird nest' shape that gives this group of ferns its common name. Unlike the common bird nest fern (A. nidus) with nearly flat, undivided fronds, A. antiquum has gently ruffled, wavy frond margins and a more refined, upright form. In humid, tropical and subtropical gardens (zones 9–11), it thrives outdoors in shade under large trees, along shaded paths, and in understory gardens, creating a bold, architectural statement with its large, glossy fronds. As a houseplant or shade container plant, it is equally outstanding. Grow in acidic, well-drained potting mix that retains moisture; never allow the root ball to dry out completely, but avoid waterlogged conditions. The plant is virtually pest-free in outdoor conditions in warm, humid climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Shade Ferns

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, coarse, airy, strongly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) epiphytic potting mix in bright indirect light to dappled shade; anthurium grows as an epiphyte in the wild and requires excellent aeration and drainage around the roots — heavy, compacted potting media causes rapid root rot.

directions: Plant in coarse, airy, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) epiphytic potting mix (orchid bark, perlite, and peat works well) in bright indirect light; allow the top inch of growing medium to dry between waterings. Anthurium andraeanum (flamingo flower) is the classic potted tropical plant with extraordinary, long-lasting blooms — a Colombian and Ecuadorian native producing the most distinctive flower structure in tropical horticulture: a glossy, heart-shaped, waxy 'spathe' (modified leaf) 3–5 inches across in red, pink, white, orange, purple, or bicolor — often described as looking plastic or lacquered — from which a finger-like yellow to white 'spadix' protrudes; the spathe and spadix together resemble a stylized flamingo or a hand with a pointed finger. Individual blooms last 2–3 months on the plant and 2–4 weeks as cut flowers. In zones 10–12, anthurium grows as a perennial landscape plant in shade; in colder zones, it is grown as a houseplant or shaded greenhouse plant. CAUTION: All parts contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic if ingested; sap may irritate skin.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Aroids

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container stock in spring in well-drained to dry, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to full sun; snake plant is one of the most drought- and neglect-tolerant houseplants and outdoor shade plants and will survive in almost any indoor or outdoor light condition except cold temperatures below 50°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in any light condition from deep shade to full sun; water infrequently — allow soil to dry completely between waterings. Sansevieria trifasciata (snake plant or mother-in-law's tongue — now sometimes classified as Dracaena trifasciata, a separate entry) is among the most indestructible of all cultivated plants — a West African succulent producing stiff, upright, sword-shaped leaves 18–48 inches tall with distinctive horizontal gray-green and dark-green banding (which gives it the 'snake plant' name from the pattern resembling snakeskin), often bordered with yellow margins in the popular 'Laurentii' cultivar. It tolerates neglect, drought, deep shade, bright sun, low humidity, and infrequent watering with virtually no complaints — making it the number-one recommended plant for beginning gardeners and those who cannot provide consistent care. Mature, pot-bound plants occasionally produce clusters of fragrant, white, vanilla-scented flowers on long stalks. Note: Sansevieria trifasciata is taxonomically equivalent to Dracaena trifasciata; some authorities retain the Sansevieria name, others use Dracaena — plants sold in nurseries may be labeled under either name.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Indoor Plants

common_name: Mother-in-law Tongue (Dracaena Snake Plant, Bowstring Hemp)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container stock in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to full shade; Dracaena trifasciata (the current accepted name for snake plant) grows outdoors year-round only in frost-free zones 10–12 but is widely grown as a houseplant in all zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in any light condition; Dracaena trifasciata survives in deep shade but grows more slowly and maintains better leaf color with moderate indirect light. Dracaena trifasciata is the current taxonomically accepted name for the plant long known as Sansevieria trifasciata (which see for the full cultural description); the transfer from Sansevieria to Dracaena was proposed in 2017 based on molecular phylogenetic studies showing that the two genera are not distinct. Garden centers and nurseries continue to sell plants labeled under both names, and both are correct in common usage. The plant is a West African native producing stiffly upright, sword-shaped, succulent-textured leaves with horizontal dark-and-light green banding and often yellow or cream leaf margins. In zones 10–12, it grows outdoors in subtropical and tropical landscapes as a clump-forming ground layer plant; in cooler zones it is one of the most popular and low-maintenance houseplants worldwide. The fiber from the leaves (historical source of the common name 'bowstring hemp') was once used to make rope and bowstrings in West Africa.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Texas Palmetto (Mexican Palmetto, Rio Grande Palmetto)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in well-drained, moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, fertile to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Texas palmetto transplants with difficulty (large specimens require professional installation) — plant small, young specimens and let them grow in place.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun; once established, Texas palmetto is drought-tolerant and requires little supplemental water. Sabal mexicana (Texas palmetto or Mexican palmetto) is the native palm of the lower Rio Grande Valley and the southernmost tip of Texas — the most cold-hardy of the larger Sabal species and the only palm native to continental Texas. It produces a massive, solitary trunk up to 50 feet tall topped with a globular crown of large, fan-shaped (palmate), blue-green fronds 4–6 feet across on 8-to-10-foot petioles; the base of old petioles persist on the trunk as a distinctive boot-like attachment giving the trunk a characteristic rough texture. The palm is closely related to the Cabbage Palm (Sabal palmetto) of the Florida/Carolina coast, but is larger and adapted to the limestone, alkaline soils of southern Texas and northern Mexico. It is the dominant palm of the Texas Gulf Coast and Rio Grande Valley and is important as an ornamental landscape tree, wildlife food source (fruits are eaten by numerous bird species including Hooded Orioles), and cultural symbol of the Rio Grande Valley. More cold-hardy than most large palms, surviving brief drops to 15°F when established.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Palms of Texas
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Palms
